query: most common site of zygomatic arch fracture

retrieved_docs.title: Zygomatic Arch Fracture -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Most zygomatic arch fractures occur in males (~80%) in the third to fourth decades of life. [4] [5] Zygomaticomaxillary complex fractures are common, and estimates suggest that ZMC fractures account for 25% of facial fractures. [5] African Americans appear to be affected most commonly (61%-64%), with Caucasians second (18%-25%), Hispanic patients third (7%-12%), and Asians fourth (5%-7%). [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Zygomatic Arch Fracture -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The zygomatic arch includes segments of the temporal bone (posteriorly) and the zygoma (anteriorly), contributing vital to the midface's structure and aesthetic appearance. As the primary determinant of the width of the cheeks, the zygomatic arch articulates with several bones of the craniofacial skeleton and supports critical muscles such as the masseter and zygomaticus major. The zygoma and its articulations with other facial bones comprise the zygomaticomaxillary complex (ZMC), also known as the zygomatico-orbito-maxillary complex. Fractures of the zygomatic arch or any of its bony articulations can cause significant functional and cosmetic morbidity. Managing zygomatic arch and ZMC fractures should be patient-specific but may range from simple observation to open reduction with internal fixation.

retrieved_docs.title: Zygomatic Arch Fracture -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The zygoma contributes a significant portion of the inferior and lateral orbital walls. Any zygoma fracture warrants evaluating possible fractures or injury of the orbit, particularly open globe injuries. Cervical spine injury must be ruled out, as well as concomitant facial fractures, including frontal sinus, nasal, midface (eg, Lefort, naso-orbito-ethmoid), and mandible fractures. Midface trauma may result in nasal septal hematoma formation, which should be assessed and managed as necessary to avoid septal necrosis and perforation or saddle nose deformity. Lastly, malocclusion may result from mandibular or midfacial trauma and should be evaluated and addressed as well.

retrieved_docs.title: Zygomatic Arch Fracture -- Introduction -- Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The zygomatic arch is the most lateral projection of the midface; this structure plays a key role because it absorbs and dissipates traumatic forces away from the cranial base. The zygoma also contributes significantly to the inferior and lateral orbital walls. Fractures of the zygoma necessitate evaluation for concomitant fractures of the orbit. The zygoma has 4 articulations, referred to as the ZMC complex (see Images. Skull Anatomy Surface Markings and Frontal Skull and Zygomatic Bones and Left Zygomatic Bone in Situ):

retrieved_docs.title: Zygomatic Arch Fracture -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Differentiate between various types of zygomatic arch fractures, considering their anatomical variations and associated complications. Select appropriate management options based on individual patient characteristics, weighing the benefits and risks of different approaches. Assess the severity and functional impact of zygomatic arch fractures comprehensively, considering both cosmetic and functional outcomes. Identify strategies for improving coordination and communication within the interprofessional healthcare team to improve patient outcomes with zygoma fractures. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Zygomatic Arch Fracture -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Any patient with sufficient trauma to sustain a ZA or ZMC fracture should undergo a full trauma evaluation to rule out any concomitant injuries to determine the most appropriate consultations (eg, neurosurgery, ophthalmology, orthopedic surgery, plastic surgery, otolaryngology, oral-maxillofacial surgery, vascular surgery). Other than the primary craniofacial trauma surgery service (otolaryngology/oral-maxillofacial surgery/plastic surgery), the most likely consultant will be an ophthalmologist or oculoplastic surgeon because of the high rate with which ocular injuries accompany ZMC fractures.

retrieved_docs.title: Zygomatic Arch Fracture -- Treatment / Management -- Operative Management
retrieved_docs.content: Management algorithm based on Zingg's classification system of ZMC fractures as described above ( see Evaluation ): [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Zygomatic Arch Fracture -- Treatment / Management -- Order of Plating
retrieved_docs.content: There is no universally accepted plating order for multipoint fixation of ZMC fractures. During single-point fixation, the ZM suture is usually plated. The ZF suture can also be plated, although most surgeons would argue that the intraoral incision used to plate the ZM buttress is less morbid than the external incision needed to access the ZF suture. When performing two-point fixation, the ZF suture should be plated first, followed by the ZM buttress. During 3 or 4-point fixation, the ZF requires plating first, then the ZM buttress, then the infraorbital rim. If one plans to plate the ZA from the outset, it can often be plated first to ensure adequate projection of the ZMC. If concomitant repair of the orbital floor is necessary, it should take place after the zygoma has been reduced, as mobilizing the zygoma often affects the configuration of the orbital floor; if the zygoma is impacted significantly, reducing it into its anatomical position will frequently increase the size of the defect by distracting the bone segments of the orbital floor.

retrieved_docs.title: Zygomatic Arch Fracture -- Evaluation -- Classification of ZMC Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: Various classification systems have been used to categorize ZMC fractures. Below is a system proposed by Zingg et al in 1992: Type A: an incomplete zygomatic fracture that involves one articulation of the zygoma A1: zygomatic arch fracture A2: lateral orbital wall fracture A3: infraorbital rim fracture Type B: all 4 articulations are fractured (a complete tetrapod fracture), with the zygomatic bone itself remaining intact Type C: a multi-fragment zygomatic fracture, wherein all 4 articulations are fractured, and the body of the zygoma is fractured as well [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Zygomatic Arch Fracture -- Treatment / Management -- Operative Management
retrieved_docs.content: Type C (comminuted tetrapod fracture): These fractures constitute absolute indications for open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF). Treatment is similar to that used for type B fractures, with ORIF of the ZM buttress, infraorbital rim, ZF suture, zygomaticotemporal suture, and zygoma fragments as necessary.

retrieved_docs.title: Zygomatic Arch Fracture -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: The zygomatic arch, formed by contributions from the temporal bone and the zygoma, plays a pivotal role in both the midface structural integrity and aesthetic appearance. Understanding this arch's intricate anatomical and functional aspects is crucial in effectively addressing zygomatic arch fractures—a common facial injury with the potential to result in both cosmetic and functional deformities. Zygomatic arch fractures, often the second most frequent facial fractures following nasal bone fractures, will be the focal point of this educational activity.

retrieved_docs.title: Zygomatic Arch Fracture -- Introduction -- Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: Fractures of the zygomaticomaxillary complex may be called "tripod fractures." The correct terminology is "tetrapod fracture," given the 4 bony articulations of the zygoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Zygomatic Arch Fracture -- Introduction -- Muscular Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The temporalis muscle originates along the temporal line of the parietal and frontal bones and travels deep to the ZA to insert into the coronoid process of the mandible; it also has attachments to the zygoma. Depressed fractures of the zygomatic arch may impede movement of the temporalis muscle and cause trismus.

retrieved_docs.title: Zygomatic Arch Fracture -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Additional information on zygomatic arch fracture include: Existing evidence regarding the management of ZMC fractures is relatively sparse, and universally accepted management standards are lacking; attention to form and function remains crucial when evaluating and repairing such fractures. Treatment options for ZMC fractures include observation, closed reduction, or open reduction and internal fixation, depending on the severity of the fractures. The smallest incision and fixation points possible should achieve a stable reduction and fixation. There should be a low threshold for consulting ophthalmology to rule out orbital injury. There is no compelling evidence for routine antibiotic use when treating zygomatic fractures.

retrieved_docs.title: Zygomatic Arch Fracture -- Introduction -- Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: Zygomaticotemporal suture: This is the temporal process of the zygoma, which articulates with the zygomatic process of the temporal bone to form the zygomatic arch. Zygomaticomaxillary (ZM) suture: This suture line crosses the inferior orbital rim, resulting in the frequent coexistence of orbital floor fractures with ZMC fractures. The ZM suture is within the ZM buttress, a key vertical support structure within the midface. The other 2 vertical buttresses of the midface are the nasomaxillary and the pterygomaxillary buttresses. Zygomaticofrontal (ZF) suture: This is lateral to the brow and is a small suture line easily accessed via a short lateral brow incision. Properly reducing a displaced ZF suture is critical to reestablishing correct facial height. Zygomaticosphenoidal (ZS) suture: The alignment of this suture is critical when reducing a fracture to reestablish pre-traumatic orbital volume, even though this suture line is not typically fixated after reduction. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2107
retrieved_docs.content: Skull and face fractures, described in Arabic as كسر في قبو القحف, refer to injuries involving breaks in the cranial vault and the facial bones, encompassing a range of traumatic damage to the head and facial structures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2459
retrieved_docs.content: Fracture of anterior fossa and fracture of middle fossa are both classified as إِصابة داخل الجمجمة (intracranial injury). Each represents a specific type of skull‑base fracture, with the anterior fossa fracture involving the front portion of the cranial base and the middle fossa fracture affecting the central region of the cranial base. Both conditions fall under the broader category of injuries inside the skull, highlighting their shared nature as intracranial traumatic lesions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2301
retrieved_docs.content: Head and neck injury is linked to a broader category of intracranial injury (إصابة داخل الجمجمة). Within this category, a fracture of the anterior fossa and a fracture of the middle fossa are each classified as specific types of intracranial injury, highlighting the hierarchical relationship among head and neck trauma, general intracranial damage, and distinct skull‑base fracture subtypes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2615
retrieved_docs.content: Fracture of anterior fossa and fracture of middle fossa are both specific subtypes of إصابة داخل الجمجمة (intracranial injury). Likewise, إصابة الرأس المغلقة (closed head injury) is classified as a form of إصابة داخل الجمجمة. Recognizing that these distinct injuries—fractures of the anterior and middle cranial fossae as well as closed head trauma—share the common characteristic of occurring within the skull underscores their relevance for accurate diagnosis, appropriate imaging, and targeted management in patients with intracranial trauma.

This integrated framework supports comprehensive assessment, classification, and management of patients presenting with complex traumatic or toxic presentations.

Skull‑base fracture is linked to the broader, unspecified fracture of the skull or facial bones (“Fractuur van schedel of aangezichtsbeenderen, deel niet gespecificeerd”) and is localized to the facial bones, which are also the procedural site for treatment of facial fracture or dislocation. Traumatic abnormality includes fracture as a child, emphasizing fractures as common manifestations of trauma. Late effects are organized under “late effects of an unspecified injury” (الآثار المتأخرة لإصابة غير محددة), which aggregates chronic sequelae from diverse acute events. Subcategories comprise late‑effect of fracture of multiple bones, late‑effect of fracture of the spine and/or trunk without spinal cord lesion, late‑effects of lower‑limb fracture, upper‑limb fracture, femoral‑neck fracture, skull and facial‑bone

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 208
retrieved_docs.content: Head and neck injury is a broad clinical category that includes intracranial injury, of which closed head injury is a specific type, and skull‑base fractures such as fracture of the anterior fossa and fracture of the middle fossa. Intracranial injury and skull‑base fractures are subclasses of fracture, the parent concept for all skeletal breakages. Fracture also encompasses cervical vertebral fracture (cervical spine fracture with cord lesion), cervical vertebral fracture as a subtype of neck injury, and pelvic fracture, which frequently co‑occurs with cervical vertebral fractures. Cranial‑vault fracture is the parent of unspecified skull or facial bone fracture, which is located in the facial bones (obličejové kosti) and is linked to facial‑bone procedural interventions (treatment of facial fracture or dislocation). Fracture of the femoral neck, fracture of the greater tuberosity, and fractures of the lower‑limb, upper‑limb, and multiple bones are all direct descendants of the general

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 877
retrieved_docs.content: The late effect of fracture of multiple bones, the late consequences of fracture of the skull and facial bones, and the late effects of fracture of the femoral neck are all described as being associated with a fracture (كسر). In addition, the open reduction of an open shoulder dislocation with fracture of the greater tuberosity is identified as a direct morphological manifestation of a fracture (كسر). Together, these statements highlight that both delayed sequelae across various skeletal regions and specific surgical interventions are directly linked to the underlying presence of a fracture, emphasizing the need for comprehensive assessment and management of fracture‑related pathology in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2454
retrieved_docs.content: FACE INJURY TRAUMA and chest injury trauma are medical conditions that frequently co‑occur with حادثة سير (traffic accident). In patients involved in a traffic accident, clinicians should be alert to the high likelihood of both facial and chest injuries, as these trauma types commonly arise together in the context of such incidents. Recognizing this association aids in comprehensive assessment, timely diagnosis, and appropriate management of trauma patients following a traffic accident.

Specific intracranial fracture types—fracture of the anterior fossa, fracture of the middle fossa, and “كسر في قبو القحف” (fracture of the cranial vault)—are each classified as both intracranial injuries and as members of the general “كسر” (fracture) category, with the Dutch parent concept “Fractuur van schedel of aangezichtsbeenderen, deel niet gespecificeerd” linking unspecified skull or facial bone fractures to the facial‑skeletal site “obličejové kosti” (facial bones). The fracture branch further includes a wide range of skeletal injuries and their long‑term sequelae. Neck injury (إصابة في الرقبة) is a specific form of كسر في الفقرة الرقبية and, together with head and neck injury, resides under a broader injury hierarchy in which head and neck injury is the parent of neck injury. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 1010
retrieved_docs.content: The late effect of fracture of multiple bones, the late consequences of fracture of the skull and facial bones, the late effects of fracture of the lower extremities, the late effects of fracture of the upper extremities, and the late effects of fracture of the femoral neck are all described as being associated with a fracture (كسر). In addition, the open reduction of an open shoulder dislocation with fracture of the greater tuberosity is identified as a direct morphological manifestation of a fracture (كسر). Together, these concepts illustrate that both the various delayed sequelae of fractures in different anatomical regions and specific surgical procedures for fracture management are intrinsically linked to the underlying fracture pathology.
